explicit_text: I was born in Laos and lived there until 1975 when my family had to flee due to the political changes happening at that time. After escaping Laos, we spent several months in the Ubon Refugee Camp before resettling in the United States. Laos is my homeland, and even though I left under difficult circumstances, it remains a central part of my identity and community work.
implicit_text: I was born in the Southeast Asian country known for its mountainous terrain and rich cultural heritage and lived there until 1975 when my family had to flee due to the political changes happening at that time. After escaping that country, we spent several months in the Ubon Refugee Camp before resettling in the United States. That homeland is my origin, and even though I left under difficult circumstances, it remains a central part of my identity and community work.

explicit_text: In 1960, I had the opportunity to attend Chulalongkorn University in Thailand for four years while I was a Buddhist monk. This education was an important part of my life before I eventually disrobed and moved on to serve my community in other ways. Studying at Chulalongkorn University helped shape my understanding and prepared me for my future roles.

explicit_text: Thailand played a significant role in my life, especially during the time I attended Chulalongkorn University in 1960. Later, after fleeing Laos in 1975, my family stayed in a refugee camp in Ubon, which is in Thailand, for several months before moving to the United States. Thailand was a place of both education and refuge for me and my family during difficult times.

explicit_text: After fleeing Laos in 1975, my family and I lived in the Ubon Refugee Camp in Ubon Ratchathani Province for about 7 or 8 months. It was there that we were interviewed and eventually sponsored to resettle in the United States. The camp was a temporary but crucial stop on our journey to a new life, and it was also where we met Dr. Jerry, who helped us find a home in Rochester, Minnesota.

explicit_text: My family resettled in Rochester, Minnesota after leaving the refugee camp in Thailand. We lived there for 16 years, during which time my children attended school and our sponsor, Dr. Jerry, took care of us. Rochester was our first home in America, and it was where we began to rebuild our lives with the help of the community and the opportunities available.

explicit_text: After living in Rochester for many years, my family moved to the Twin Cities area. This move allowed us to be closer to a larger Lao community where I serve as a Mor Phone, officiating ceremonies and supporting cultural traditions. The Twin Cities have become an important place for continuing my community work and preserving our heritage.

This move allowed us to be closer to a larger Lao community where I serve as a Mor Phone, officiating ceremonies and supporting cultural traditions. This metropolitan area has become an important place for continuing my community work and preserving our heritage.

explicit_text: The United States became my new home after my family fled Laos and spent time in a refugee camp in Thailand. Thanks to the sponsorship of an American doctor, we resettled first in Rochester, Minnesota, and later moved to the Twin Cities. America provided us with safety, education for my children, and the chance to continue serving my community as a Mor Phone.
